questions.text: Throughout a time interval, while the speed of a particle increases as it moves along the $x$ axis, its velocity and acceleration might be
An object is shot vertically upward. While it is rising:
options: its velocity and acceleration are both upward
its velocity is upward and its acceleration is downward
its velocity and acceleration are both downward
its velocity and acceleration are both decreasing

questions.text: An object starts from rest at $x=0$ when $t=0$. The object moves along the axis with positive velocity after $t=0$. The instantaneous velocity $v_x$ and the average velocity $v_{x,av}$ are related by
options: $v_x$ can be more than, equal to, or less than $v_{x,av}$

questions.text: As a rocket is accelerating vertically upward at $9.8~\text{m/s}^2$ near Earth's surface, it releases a projectile. Immediately after release the acceleration (in $\text{m/s}^2$) of the projectile is

A particle is at rest. At $t=0$, it is given an acceleration that, beginning with $3.0~\text{m/s}^2$ at $t=0$, decreases linearly to zero in 8.0 s and then remains zero. What will be the maximum speed of the particle?
A feather, initially at rest, is released in a vacuum 12 m above the surface of the earth. Which of the following statements is correct?
options: The maximum velocity of the feather is $9.8~\text{m/s}$
The acceleration of the feather increases during the fall.
The acceleration of the feather is zero.
The acceleration of the feather remains constant during the fall.

questions.text: An object has a constant acceleration of $3~\text{m/s}^2$. The position versus time graph for this object has a slope

Two particles move in a straight line towards each other at initial velocities $v_1$ and $v_2$ and with accelerations $a_1$ and $a_2$, respectively, directed against the corresponding initial velocities. The maximum initial separation between the particles, so that they meet subsequently, is
An object is moving along the $x$ axis with its position as a function of time given by $x=x(t)$. Point O is at $x=0$. The object is definitely moving towards O when

A ball is dropped from a certain height from the floor. Each time it hits the floor, it rises to half its original height. Take the vertically upward direction as the +y direction and the floor as the origin of the coordinates. Which of the following graphs correctly represent the motion of the ball? ($v_y$: velocity, $v$: speed, $y$: position, $t$: time)
A baseball is thrown vertically into the air. The acceleration of the ball at its highest point is
An object is moving in the x direction with velocity $v_x$ and $\dfrac{dv_x}{dt}$ is a non-zero constant. If $v_x=0$ when $t=0$ then for $t>0$ the quantity $v_x \dfrac{dv_x}{dt}$ is

At a stop light, a truck travelling at $15~\text{m/s}$ passes a car as it starts from rest. The truck travels at constant velocity and the car accelerates at $3~\text{m/s}^2$. How much time (in second) does the car take to catch up to the truck?
The coordinate of an object is given as a function of time by $x=7t-3t^2$ where $x$ is in meters and $t$ is in seconds. Its average velocity over the interval from $t=0$ to $t=4$ is
Which one of the following statements is correct for an object released from rest?
options: The average velocity during the first second of time is $4.9~\text{m/s}$.
During each second the object falls $9.8~\text{m}$.
The object falls $9.8~\text{m}$ during the first second of time.
The acceleration changes by $9.8~\text{m/s}^2$ every second.

questions.text: A car moving on a straight highway travelled the first one-third of its journey at a speed of $10~\text{km/h}$, the second one-third at $20~\text{km/h}$ and the final one-third at $60~\text{km/h}$. The magnitude of the average velocity of the car is
A heavy ball falls freely, starting from rest. If $g=10~\text{m/s}^2$ then between the third and fourth second of time it travels a distance of
Which of the following graphs CANNOT possibly represent the motion of a particle moving along the $x$ axis? ($x$: position, $v_x$: velocity, v: speed, s: distance, t: time.)

Each of four particles move along the x axis. Their coordinates (in meters) as functions of time (in seconds) are given by
particle 1: $x(t)=3.5-2.7t^3$
particle 2: $x(t)=3.5+2.7t^3$
particle 3: $x(t)=3.5+2.7t^2$
particle 4: $x(t)=3.5-3.4t-2.7t^2$
Which of these particles have constant acceleration?
The acceleration of an object, starting from rest, is shown in the graph below. Other than at $t=0$ when is the velocity of the object equal to zero?
options: At no other time less than or equal to 5s

questions.text: A paratrooper jumps from an aeroplane and opens a parachute after 2 s of free fall and starts decelerating at 3 m/s$^2$. At 10 m height from ground, while descending with the help of parachute, the speed of paratrooper is 5 m/s. What is the initial height of the airplane?
A person travelling at $43.2~\text{km/h}$ applies the brakes giving a deceleration of $6.0~\text{m/s}^2$ to his scooter. How far will it travel (in meters) before stopping?

A bullet travelling with a velocity of $16~\text{m/s}$ penetrates a tree trunk and comes to rest after moving 40 cm. Find the time taken (in seconds) by the bullet to stop assuming that the tree trunk provides a uniform resistance.

A train starts from rest and moves with a constant acceleration of $2.0~\text{m/s}^2$ for half a minute. The brakes are then applied and the train comes to rest in one minute. Let the total distance moved by the train be $s$ meters and the maximum speed attained during the motion be $v$ m/s. Then $s+v$ equals:

A point moving with uniform acceleration covers in the last second of its motion $\dfrac{9}{25}\text{th}$ of the whole distance. If it started from rest and covers 3 m in the first second, find how long it was in motion (in seconds).

A point moving with uniform acceleration covers 25 m in the half second duration immediately after the first second of its motion, and 198 m in the eleventh second of its motion. Find the magnitude of the acceleration of the point (in $\text{m/s}^2$).
A particle starts from rest and moves with a uniform acceleration of $2~\text{m/s}^2$. Find the time taken (in seconds) by it to cover the third meter.

A particle initially at rest starts moving from reference point $x=0$ along the $x$-axis, with velocity $v$ that varies as $v= 4\sqrt{x}$ m/s. What is the distance (in meter) travelled by the particle in 4 seconds?

A particle is moving along the $x$ axis. The variation of its position $x$ (in m) with time $t$ (in s) is given as $x=-3t^3+18t^2+16t+16$. The velocity of the particle (in m/s) when its acceleration becomes zero is:

The displacement and the increase in the velocity of a particle in the time interval of $t$ to $t+1$ s are 125 m and 50 m/s, respectively. The distance travelled by the particle in the $(t+2)$-th s (in m) is:
